title: *The Quiet Cure*
blurb: A framed surgeon. A city that hungers for order. In Prohibition’s shadow, one woman will let the underworld burn to reclaim her name.
comparable: For fans of *The Lies of Locke Lamora* by Scott Lynch, *The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue* by V.E. Schwab

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Sunset Grill’s back stairwell smelled of lemon oil and cigarette tar. Nora’s heels clicked sharp against the wooden steps, each one steady even as the bass from the club thrummed through the floorboards below. She adjusted the cuffs of her silk blouse, then rapped twice on the door marked *Private* in peeling gold leaf.

Reggie glanced up from the ledger. His fingers stilled over the adding machine, the ink ribbon curling like a dead thing. “Knife fight,” he said. “Piano man’s shaking worse than the gin I had last night.”

The door pushed open before she could reply. A kid in a bow tie stumbled backward, pressing a bloodied handkerchief to his side. His shirt was dark at the ribs, his breath coming in shallow gasps.

Nora stepped over the threshold, shedding her coat onto the coatrack. The clinic was small: an exam table under a single bare bulb, a wall of labeled jars, and a desk cluttered with receipts. The air smelled of antiseptic and old whiskey.

She didn’t touch the boy. “Sit. Don’t lean.”

Reggie didn’t look up as he reached for the ledger. His fountain pen hovered over the first blank line, the nib trembling like a tuning fork.

The piano man hesitated. “I ain’t got—”

“Money’s not the issue,” Nora said. “Sit.”

He obeyed, perching on the edge of the exam table with his good hand gripping the metal frame. Nora rolled a stool close, then uncapped a bottle of iodine. The scent cut through the room.

“Stitching’s gonna scar,” she said. “You play after this, the scar’ll pull. Won’t matter if it’s jazz or hymns, it’ll sound like a cat fight.”

The piano man swallowed. “I need it clean. My fingers—”

“You need them attached.” Nora uncapped a syringe. “Hold still.”

Reggie’s pen scratched across the ledger. The sound was too loud in the quiet room. Nora didn’t look up, but she felt the shift in the air—Reggie’s tension, the way his wrist flickered before pressing harder into the paper.

“Local first,” she said to the piano man. “Then a little something to keep you from twitching like a plucked string.”

The piano man nodded, but his eyes darted to Reggie. A bead of sweat ran from his temple to his jaw.

Reggie cleared his throat. “Doc, the ledger—”

“Not now.” Nora injected the lidocaine, watching the tension leave the piano man’s shoulders. “You’ll feel pressure, not pain. Breathe.”

The piano man exhaled, slow and shaky. “You always say that.”

“Because it’s true.” She threaded the needle. Her hands didn’t shake. “And because people clench when they’re scared. Mouth, fists, whole damn body. Makes the work harder.”

Reggie’s ledger snapped shut. The sound made the piano man flinch.

“There,” Nora said, tying off the last stitch. “Keep it dry. No heavy playing for a week. If it opens, come back. If it festers, don’t.”

The piano man stood, wincing as the wound pulled. He dropped a nickel onto the desk—enough for the gin he’d drink before he hit the street.

Reggie slid the nickel into the ledger as payment, then turned the page. His hand hovered over a name scrawled in pencil just that morning: *Lieutenant Vito Moretti*.

The piano man lingered in the doorway. “Doc… you ever patch a bullet?”

Nora’s fingers stilled. Reggie’s shoulders locked.

“No,” Nora said. “I don’t.”

The piano man nodded, like that made sense. “Thought not.”

The door swung shut behind him.

Reggie exhaled through his nose. His pen hovered over Moretti’s name, the ledger open like an invitation.

Nora wiped her hands on a towel. “The audit’s Friday. Don’t let me forget the receipts for the antiseptic.”

Reggie’s pen didn’t move. The silence stretched, thick as the iodine fumes.

Outside, the piano man’s fingers found the keys. A slow, mournful chord seeped through the floorboards.

Nora looked up.

A woman stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the stairwell’s dim light. She wore a mannish suit and a trumpet case slung over one shoulder. Her fingers were long, the nails short and unpainted. A faint scar ran from her wrist to the base of her thumb.

Nora knew that scar.

Lila Chase.

The woman’s eyes locked onto Nora’s hands—still gloved in crimson, still steady. Then Lila’s gaze lifted, slow, deliberate, meeting Nora’s.

“Played that one at your funeral,” Lila said. “Sounded wrong without you.”

Reggie’s pen finally moved. The ledger page flipped with a whisper.

Nora pulled off her gloves. “You’re early, Lila.”

Lila stepped inside. The door clicked shut behind her.

The needle in Reggie’s hand hovered over Moretti’s name.

Chapter 2

The office smelled of lemon oil and old papers when Nora arrived at seven-thirty. Reggie had already aired the place out. The front door stood wide to the morning chill, the brass bell silent above it. Two federal inspectors in identical gray suits sat at the patient chairs, their hats on their knees, their gloves folded precisely. Between them, Alden Graves leaned against the desk, one ankle crossed over the other, a man who had never once doubted himself in his life.

Reggie’s pencil moved across the ledger like a metronome set to slow. He didn’t look up when Nora stepped inside. Neither did the inspectors.

Graves smiled. “Dr. Voss. You’re late.”

“Am I?” Nora hung her coat on the rack. The wood creaked under her fingers. “The clock says seven-thirty. My appointment book says the same.”

One inspector coughed into his fist. The other adjusted his tie.

Graves pushed off the desk. “Federal records say seven. You were scheduled for seven.”

“Federal records are wrong.” Nora opened the cabinet beneath the sink, pulled out a stack of fresh towels. “Reggie, the receipts for last week’s catgut and iodine.”

Reggie turned a page. The ledger’s spine cracked like a gunshot in the quiet.

Graves’ smile didn’t waver. “I’ll be taking your license today.”

Nora folded the towels. “On what grounds?”

“Unlicensed practice. Evidence tampering. Obstruction of federal oversight.” Graves gestured to the inspectors. “They have a warrant.”

The first inspector cleared his throat. “Dr. Voss, you’re hereby ordered to surrender all medical supplies, records, and instruments pending investigation.”

Reggie’s pencil snapped.

Nora didn’t look at him. She stacked the towels with deliberate care. “Reggie.”

He didn’t answer. His face was pale, his jaw tight.

Graves reached into his coat. A folded paper came out, crisp as a newly minted dollar. He slapped it onto the desk. “You’ll sign where the X is.”

Nora read the warrant. Her name was printed at the top. Below it, in small type: *Evidence of gross malpractice—bloodstained surgical instruments, unregistered narcotics, falsified patient records.*

She folded the paper in half. “No.”

Graves’ fingers twitched. “No?”

“No,” Nora said. “I won’t sign away my livelihood based on innuendo.”

One inspector stood. His chair legs scraped the floor. “Dr. Voss, this is obstruction. It adds twenty years.”

Nora folded the paper again. “Twenty years for what, exactly? Refusing to surrender tools I use to deliver babies in alleys? Refusing to sign a document that calls me a criminal without a trial?” She looked at Graves. “You’re a deputy superintendent. Does this feel like justice to you?”

Graves’ smile flickered. For the first time, something like irritation crossed his face. “Justice has a price, Doctor. Pay it.”

Reggie’s chair scraped back. He stood, the ledger clutched to his chest. “Alden, you can’t do this.”

“Watch me.” Graves’ voice was quiet. “Reggie, the ledger. Now.”

Reggie’s knuckles turned white. “It’s not here.”

Graves’ eyes flicked to the desk, the cabinet, the coatrack. “Then where is it?”

“Safe.” Reggie’s voice didn’t shake. “Not here. Not yours.”

Graves reached out. His hand hovered over Reggie’s shoulder. “Don’t force me to empty this room stone by stone.”

Reggie stepped back. The ledger pressed against his chest like a shield.

Nora watched the exchange, her pulse steady. She turned to the inspectors. “Gentlemen, if you’ll excuse me, I need to retrieve my instruments.”

The first inspector didn’t move. “You’re not permitted to touch anything.”

“Then arrest me.” Nora’s voice was soft. “Or step aside.”

A beat of silence. Then the second inspector nodded to the first. They moved in unison, flanking the cabinet beneath the sink. The first inspector knelt, the second stood with his hands clasped behind his back.

Graves stepped closer to Nora. Close enough that she could smell the starch in his shirt. “Your scalpel collection. I know you keep them in that cabinet.”

Nora didn’t answer.

Graves reached past her, pulled open the cabinet door. The wooden trays slid out, each one lined with gleaming steel. Scalpels, forceps, retractors—all sterilized, all accounted for in Reggie’s ledger.

Graves plucked a monogrammed handle from the third tray. The steel gleamed under the single bulb. The monogram was V.M.

Reggie’s breath hitched.

Graves turned the scalpel slowly. The ring embedded in the handle caught the light—a thick gold band, melted into the steel like a brand.

“Vito Moretti,” Graves said. “Shot through the heart before this scalpel ever touched him. And yet here it is, in your cabinet, bloodstained and untouched by time.”

The inspectors froze.

Reggie’s mouth opened. Closed.

Nora didn’t look at the scalpel. She kept her gaze on Graves’ face. “That’s a lie.”

“Is it?” Graves held the scalpel up. The bloodstain was dark, old, shaped like a teardrop. “Or is it the truth you’ve been hiding behind silk gloves and whispered consultations?”

The second inspector reached for his cuffs. The first stayed kneeling, his eyes locked onto the cabinet.

Reggie’s voice cracked. “Nora, what the hell is this?”

Nora didn’t answer him. She kept her eyes on Graves. “You have no proof. No witness. No motive. Just a scalpel with a ring melted into it and a ledger Reggie never saw before today.”

Graves laughed. A dry, humorless sound. “The ledger’s already filed with the federal auditors. They’ll find the payments. The cover-ups. The unlicensed procedures.” He leaned closer. “You’re finished, Doctor.”

Reggie’s hands trembled. He set the ledger on the desk, snapped it shut. The sound made the inspectors flinch.

Nora turned to the cabinet. She picked up the tray with the monogrammed handle. The blade was cold against her palm.

Graves reached for it. “That’s evidence.”

Nora closed her fingers around the handle. The ring pressed into her skin. “No. It’s mine.”

Graves’ hand shot out, gripping her wrist. His fingers were strong, his nails sharp. “You’re under arrest.”

Nora didn’t pull away. She let him see her face—calm, unflinching. Then she did the one thing no one expected.

She dropped the tray.

The scalpel clattered against the cabinet’s metal lip, then slid to the floor with a high, discordant ring. The ring embedded in the handle snapped off, skittered across the linoleum like a startled insect.

Graves’ grip on her wrist tightened. “You fool.”

Nora didn’t react. Her eyes were on the door.

It opened.

Sunlight spilled in, blinding for a moment. Then the light sharpened, and a woman stood there—a trumpet case slung over her shoulder, a mannish suit hugging her frame. Her eyes locked onto Nora’s, then flickered to Graves’ hand on Nora’s wrist.

Lila Chase.

Graves didn’t let go. His fingers bit into Nora’s skin. “Who the hell are you?”

Lila didn’t answer. She stepped inside, her boots clicking against the floor. Her gaze never left Nora’s face.

Reggie’s voice was a rasp. “Lila.”

Graves finally released Nora. He stepped back, smoothing his coat. “Another conspirator, I suppose.”

Lila ignored him. She looked at Nora, her expression unreadable. Then she crouched, picked up the ring from the floor. Held it between two fingers like something dead.

Reggie’s breath came fast. “Lila, what the hell are you doing here?”

She stood. Turned the ring in her fingers. The gold caught the light. “Same thing as Graves. Cleaning up.”

Graves’ jaw tightened. “You’re the trumpeter from the Sunset Grill.”

Lila didn’t answer. She slipped the ring into her pocket. Then she reached into her trumpet case, pulled out a stack of sheet music. The edges were dog-eared, the margins filled with Nora’s precise handwriting—notes, patient records, dates.

She dropped the case.

The music scattered across the floor, fluttering like wounded birds. Some pages landed face-up. Others spun, revealing Nora’s handwriting in stark black ink.

Graves’ eyes flicked to the pages. Then to Nora.

Nora didn’t move. She didn’t breathe.

Graves crouched, picked up a page. His lips moved as he read. Then his face went still.

Reggie’s voice was a whisper. “Nora… what is that?”

Nora didn’t answer.

Graves stood. His face was gray. “This is a patient log.”

Lila’s voice cut through the silence. “Should’ve brought a lawyer, Graves. But you never listen to the music first.”

The inspectors stood frozen, their cuffs unused.

Graves looked at Nora. Then at Lila. Then back at the pages.

Reggie’s voice cracked. “For God’s sake, Nora—”

Nora’s eyes were on Lila. “You were always early.”

Lila didn’t smile. She didn’t flinch. She just held Nora’s gaze, then turned and walked out.

The door clicked shut behind her.

Graves’ voice was a blade. “Detain her.”

The first inspector moved toward the door.

Nora didn’t stop him.

She waited until the door swung open, then closed again. Then she knelt, picked up a page of sheet music. Her fingers were steady.

Reggie’s voice was raw. “Nora, what just happened?”

She tucked the page into her coat pocket. Then she stood, walked to the cabinet, and pulled a fresh towel from the stack.

She began wiping down the counter, her movements precise, her back to the room.

Reggie’s hand landed on her shoulder. “Nora.”

She didn’t turn. “Reggie. The receipts for last week’s antiseptic. I need them before the auditors get here.”

Reggie’s fingers tightened. “We have to talk about this.”

“Later.” Nora kept wiping. “After the inspection.”

Graves’ voice was tight. “This isn’t over.”

Nora didn’t answer. She folded the towel, laid it on the counter.

Then she walked past Graves, past the inspectors, her heels clicking against the floor.

Reggie didn’t move. His hand hung in the air where her shoulder had been.

The office was silent except for the faint sound of feet on the stairs outside.

Reggie lowered his hand.

Then he walked to the desk, opened the ledger, and started writing.

Chapter 3

The clinic above the Sunset Grill was too quiet. Nora sat on the piano bench, fingers hovering over the keys, not playing. The ivory stared back at her, blank as a ledger page. Below, the club pulsed—laughter, the low thrum of double bass, the sharp crack of a snare. Up here, only the hum of the radiator and the distant wail of a trumpet rehearsing a solo.

Reggie stood in the doorway, coat still on, hat in hand. His breath fogged in the cold air. “You’re not answering your phone.”

Nora didn’t look up. “It’s not mine to answer.”

“It’s mine.” He stepped inside, shut the door behind him. The brass knob clicked like a gun’s hammer. “The girls at the switchboard say you’ve got three messages from the guild.”

“Tell them I’m dead.” Her fingers brushed the black keys. One note, soft and sour.

Reggie didn’t laugh. He set his hat on the windowsill, where the winter light cut a pale rectangle across the wood. “Graves is at the Green Canary right now. Giving a speech. Calling you a butcher in front of every reporter in the city.”

Nora’s thumb hovered over middle C. “Let him.”

“He’s burning your name.” Reggie’s voice dropped. “And mine. He’s got the ledger, Nora. Or at least what he thinks is the ledger.”

She played a single arpeggio—fingers light, mechanical. The sound trembled in the still air.

Reggie exhaled through his nose. “You buried the real one.”

No question. Just a statement.

Nora lifted her hands from the keys. “Reggie.”

He stepped closer, his shoes squeaking on the varnished floor. “You buried it in the alley wall. The east side, near the dumpster. A loose brick, third row from the top.”

She turned on the bench. Her coat whispered against the wood. “You shouldn’t know that.”

“Because I shouldn’t look?” His jaw set. “I’ve been your office manager for twelve years, Nora. I know when you’re lying. I know when you’re hiding.”

“Do you?” She stood. The piano bench scraped back. “Then you know this isn’t over.”

He laughed once, sharp. “It’s barely begun. Graves has the scalpel, Nora. The one with the ring. It’s enough to bury you.”

“It’s not.” She moved past him, toward the cabinet in the corner. A medical kit sat half-unpacked on the counter—gauze, iodine, a pair of forceps. She picked up the forceps, tested the grip.

Reggie watched her. “What aren’t you telling me?”

Nora set the forceps down. She walked to the back wall, knelt. Brushed dust from the baseboard with her fingertips. A brick was loose. She pulled it free.

A small ledger lay inside, wrapped in oilcloth. She unwrapped it, flipped open the cover. The pages smelled of mildew and old ink. Names, dates, payments—none of them hers.

Reggie crouched beside her. His shadow fell across the pages. “What is this?”

“Insurance.” She turned a page. Capone’s signature at the top. Illegal whiskey shipments listed by the barrel. A running tally of payments to the West Side Medical Guild.

Reggie’s fingers whitened around the edge of the oilcloth. “You kept *their* records?”

Nora didn’t answer. She flipped to the last page. A single entry, recent: *V.M. – full removal. No questions.*

Reggie went still. “Moretti.”

Nora closed the ledger. Wrapped it back in oilcloth. Slid it into the space where the brick had been. Pushed the brick back into place.

She stood. Brushed her knees clean.

Reggie stayed kneeling. His voice was quiet. “You were going to let Graves win.”

Nora walked to the piano. Laid her palm on the lid. “I never lose.”

“Not like this.” He stood, brushed his own knees. “He’s got the scalpel, Nora. The bloodstain. The ledger you *didn’t* bury. He’s got enough to convict you by sundown.”

She didn’t turn. “Then he’ll have to try.”

Reggie stepped in front of her. His face was pale under the yellowed bulb. “You’re planning something.”

She met his eyes. “I’m always planning.”

He grabbed her wrist. Not hard. Just enough to anchor her. “What aren’t you planning for?”

Nora looked down at his hand. Then back at his face. She didn’t pull away.

Reggie’s breath hitched. “Lila’s testimony.”

Nora’s pulse didn’t change.

Reggie swallowed. “She’s the only one who can place you somewhere else that night. Playing piano. Not stitching a dead man.”

Nora stayed silent.

Reggie’s fingers tightened. “She’s out there. Somewhere. Waiting.”

Nora reached up. Gently, she pried his fingers from her wrist. His hands fell to his sides, empty.

Then she walked to the cabinet. Pulled a clean towel from the stack. Folded it with slow, deliberate care.

Reggie didn’t move. His mouth opened. Closed.

Nora draped the towel over the handle of the medical cabinet.

Turned toward the door.

Reggie’s voice cracked. “Nora.”

She didn’t look back. Opened the door. Stepped into the hall.

The stairs creaked under her heels. Down, down, into the warm belly of the Sunset Grill. The music swallowed her footsteps.

Reggie stood in the clinic doorway, his mouth open, his hands hanging at his sides like he’d forgotten how to use them.

Then the door swung shut behind her.

He didn’t follow.

He just stood there, staring at the space where she’d been.

And the ledger in the wall stayed where it was buried.

Chapter 4

The Green Canary’s back room smelled of cigar smoke and pine tar. Graves stood behind a lectern draped in emerald velvet, his back to the two dozen reporters and city officials jammed into the space. The heat from the radiator made his collar itch. He kept his jacket on.

A lieutenant in a tan overcoat leaned in from the side door. No knock. Just a slip of an envelope between stiff fingers, followed by a nod sharp enough to draw blood. Graves didn’t take his eyes off the room.

“Thank you, Captain.” He let the envelope rest on the lectern’s edge, within sight but not yet claimed. “As I was saying—”

“Sir.” The lieutenant’s voice didn’t rise. Didn’t need to. The room stilled anyway. “You’ll want this before you finish.”

Graves exhaled through his nose. The envelope was sealed with red wax, stamped with the insignia of the 21st Precinct. Not Capone. Worse. Internal. He broke the seal with his thumbnail, unfolded the single sheet.

Mercy Row Alley. 11:47 PM. Witness recalls more than planned.

No signature. No rank. Just the threat of someone talking too much. Graves folded the paper back into the envelope. Slid it into his breast pocket beside the folded warrant for Nora Voss.

“Gentlemen,” he said, “I’ve just received confirmation that the city’s most dangerous unlicensed physician has been identified by at least one credible witness. Tonight, we move from surveillance to arrest.”

A reporter in a moth-eaten coat raised a hand. “Deputy Superintendent, how do you respond to accusations that you’re targeting women healers simply because they’re not affiliated with the new hospital board?”

Graves smiled. Cold. Calculated. “Because, Mr. O’Malley, I have no quarrel with women healers. I have every quarrel with butchers who leave bodies in alleys and call it medicine.”

The room laughed. The lieutenant behind him shifted, boots squeaking on the varnished floor.

Graves pressed on. “Last week, two children in the West Town district were treated for laudanum poisoning by an unlicensed practitioner using unsterilized instruments. The same so-called doctor has patched three Capone soldiers with bullet wounds incompatible with survival. One died on the table. Another is currently in federal custody, bleeding out from an infection introduced during an ‘emergency procedure.’”

He paused. Let the room absorb it.

“We will not wait for another grave mistake. By week’s end, every unlicensed clinic in this city will be shuttered. Every healer operating without a license will be prosecuted. And every patient who trusts their life to a backroom surgeon will be warned.”

A woman in a cloche hat—city desk, not tabloid—called out, “What about the charges against Nora Voss, Deputy Superintendent? Is it true she refused to stitch a Capone lieutenant who later died of his wounds?”

Graves didn’t flinch. “It is true that Dr. Voss was present when Michael Rizzo sustained a fatal gunshot wound. It is also true that she chose not to render aid despite her professional obligation to do so. Then, inexplicably, she vanished from her office and left the patient unattended. Rizzo bled out in Mercy Row Alley.”

The reporter scribbled furiously.

Graves lifted a file from the lectern. Thin. Neat. Evidence. “The medical examiner confirms the wound trajectory matches the fatal shot. The scalpel recovered from Dr. Voss’s office bears Rizzo’s monogrammed ring fused to the blade. And just this morning, federal auditors uncovered a ledger in her name detailing payments from known criminals over the past three years.”

The room buzzed. Graves allowed himself a single satisfied breath.

The lieutenant stepped forward again, this time holding a ledger bound in black leather. Not the copy. The original. Its pages fanned open like a fan of aces. Graves caught a glimpse—Capone’s signature, whiskey runs, payments to “V.M.” in column after column.

He didn’t take it. Not yet. He wanted the moment to linger.

“This,” he said, gesturing to the ledger, “is the ledger of the West Side Medical Guild. It proves a network of black-market surgeons operating across the city, prioritizing profit over patients. Dr. Voss was not alone. She was merely the most visible. The most reckless.”

He turned to the lieutenant. “Burn it.”

The room gasped. The lieutenant didn’t hesitate. He struck a match against the lectern’s edge. The flame caught the corner of the ledger. Orange spread like spilled whiskey. The smell of burning paper and ink filled the room.

Graves watched the flames lick the pages. The names curled. The numbers disappeared. The evidence of his own corruption vanished in an instant.

He smiled.

Then the lieutenant leaned close, his voice a blade against Graves’s ear. “Sir. The witness. Lila Chase.”

Graves froze. One finger twitched.

“She remembers more. She wants to talk.”

Graves turned slowly. The firelight painted the lieutenant’s face in amber and shadow. “You let her talk to you?”

“Not me, sir. One of ours. She played at the Sunset Grill last night. Said she saw Voss at the piano all night. Not in Mercy Row.”

Graves exhaled through his teeth. “Then why the hell didn’t you bring her in?”

The lieutenant’s eyes flicked toward the door. A hand signal. Recognition. Worse.

Graves turned.

At the back of the room, standing in the dim glow of a wall sconce, was a woman in a green dress, the color of old money and colder decisions. She held a trumpet case in one hand, a cigarette in the other. Smoke curled from the tip.

Lila Chase.

Her gaze locked onto Graves’s.

She took a slow drag. Let the smoke drip from her lips.

Graves felt the room tilt. The ledger fire died behind him. The warrant in his pocket felt like a stone.

She stepped forward, heels clicking on the oak floor.

Graves didn’t wait. He moved toward her, hand extended as if to shake, as if this were a social call.

“Miss Chase,” he said. “We haven’t met. I’m Deputy Superintendent Alden Graves.”

She didn’t take his hand. She tapped ash onto his shoe.

“You’re a man who burns evidence in front of reporters,” she said. “I don’t shake hands with arsonists.”

Graves laughed. It came out thin. “Then let’s talk about your testimony.”

She exhaled smoke between them. “No.”

The room had gone still. Reporters leaned in. A photographer lifted his camera.

Graves kept his voice low. “You were there the night Rizzo died. You heard the shot. You saw Dr. Voss. You can place her elsewhere.”

Lila stepped closer. Close enough that he could see the gold flecks in her dark eyes. Close enough that the scent of her perfume—jasmine and sweat—filled his nose.

“I was playing piano,” she said. “All night. Ask the boys from the Sunset. Ask the bouncer. Ask the bartender. They’ll all tell you the same thing.”

Graves felt the envelope in his pocket burn against his thigh. He wanted to rip it open. Throw it in the fire. Pretend it never existed.

“And yet,” he said, “you haven’t come forward.”

Lila flicked her cigarette onto the floor. Ground it out with her heel. The ember died with a hiss.

“Because I don’t owe Nora Voss anything,” she said. “She cut me open once to save my hand. I bled on her floor. I woke up with stitches and a scar and a debt I could never repay. So no. I don’t owe her. But I also don’t owe you.”

Graves forced a smile. “You owe the truth.”

She laughed. Real. Unfiltered. The kind that made heads turn.

“Truth?” She leaned in. Her voice dropped. “The truth is, Deputy Superintendent, you’re standing in a room full of liars. And you don’t even know which ones are yours.”

She turned on her heel. Walked toward the exit. Her trumpet case tapped against her leg with every step.

Graves didn’t call her back.

He stood there, the heat from the dying fire at his back, the weight of the sealed envelope in his pocket, and the knowledge that the one witness who could save his promotion was walking out the door.

The lieutenant leaned in again. “Sir?”

Graves didn’t answer. He watched Lila Chase disappear into the hallway. Watched the door swing shut behind her.

Then he reached into his pocket. Tore open the envelope.

Inside, on a single sheet of paper, in ink that hadn’t been there before:

*Her name is Lila Chase. She plays piano at the Sunset Grill. She was at the scene. She knows where the real ledger is buried.*

Graves crushed the paper in his fist.

The lieutenant’s voice was a whisper. “Sir.”

Graves looked up.

The lieutenant was holding out a fresh envelope. This one had no wax seal. No insignia. Just a name scrawled across the front in shaky handwriting:

*Nora Voss.*

Chapter 5

The Blackstone Hotel ballroom gleamed under chandeliers that dripped crystal like frozen rain. Doctors in starched collars murmured behind program sheets, their breath fogging the air despite the roaring steam pipes. Nora stood at the back, gloved fingers curled around a leather portfolio, her dark dress swallowing the dim light.

Graves stood at the lectern, his voice smoothing over the room like varnish. "Suture," he said, dragging the word into three syllables. A resident in the third row cleared his throat.

"Hyphenated, Superintendent," the man called out. "Soo-cher."

Graves’s smile didn’t waver. "Of course. A minor point in the grand pursuit of public health." His gaze skated over the crowd, missing Nora entirely.

She watched his left hand. The way his fingers tapped the lectern in a rhythm that wasn’t quite a beat, wasn’t quite a pulse. He was nervous. Good.

A waiter in a brass-buttoned coat passed with a tray of coffee and brandy. Nora accepted a thimble of rye. The liquid burned clean. She exhaled through her nose and let the glass dangle from her fingertips.

Graves droned on about municipal sanitation. "The body is a temple," he intoned, "and the physician its high priest." The room tittered. Nora allowed herself a sip of water. It tasted of rust.

She drifted toward the side aisle, heels muted by the plush carpet. The doctor beside her adjusted his pince-nez and leaned toward his neighbor. "Did you hear? The County Hospital board just approved his funding."

The neighbor exhaled cigarette smoke through her pinched lips. "He’ll have them rubber-stamp his promotion before spring."

Nora’s smile stayed private. She turned at the far wall, running her palm along the wainscoting. The wood was cool beneath her fingers. Safe. Hidden.

Graves’s voice sharpened. "Unsanctioned practices endanger not only the patient but the very fabric of civic trust." His eyes flicked toward the exit.

Nora followed his gaze. A woman in a cloche coat scribbled notes with a silver pencil. A man in a seersucker suit shifted his weight, jingling coins in his pocket.

Reggie would call them plants. Graves called them progress.

Nora let her fingertips trail the edge of an oak bookshelf. A medical text lay open to a plate of arterial sutures. She studied the knots—too loose, too flashy for real work. Graves’s handiwork, not a surgeon’s.

She closed the book with a snap.

The crowd murmured. Graves paused, mid-sentence. His gaze snagged on something beyond the podium. A nurse in a starched cap had dropped a tray of steel instruments. Scalpels clattered, scattered like teeth on the floor.

Graves’s mouth tightened.

The nurse dropped to her knees, hands fluttering over the scattered tools. "Apologies, Superintendent."

Graves waved her off. "Leave them."

He stepped forward, polished shoe extended toward the closest blade. The metal caught the chandelier light—a silver teardrop engraved with an entwined G and C.

Nora’s breath caught.

Graves knelt, picked up the scalpel between thumb and forefinger. He turned it, inspecting the monogram. "Odd," he murmured. "This looks like—"

A floorboard creaked behind Nora. She didn’t turn. She smelled carbolic acid and lavender before she heard the whisper of silk against wool.

"Miss Chase," Graves said. His voice had gained a new edge, sharp enough to cut glass.

Nora turned.

Lila stood three feet away, trumpet case hooked over her shoulder, a cigarette holder between her fingers. Smoke curled from the tip. She hadn’t played a note tonight—she wore a velvet gown the color of dried blood.

Graves didn’t acknowledge Nora. His focus stayed on Lila. "You’re the trumpeter from the Sunset Grill."

Lila exhaled smoke through her nose. "I’m the one who played last night. Yes."

Graves stood, scalpel still in hand. "You were present when Michael Rizzo sustained his fatal wound."

Lila flicked ash onto the floor. "I was playing piano."

"All night."

"All night."

Graves closed his fingers around the scalpel. "You are aware this is a murder weapon."

Lila’s eyes flicked to Nora, then back to Graves. "I’m aware you’re holding it."

Nora felt the room tilt. The air between them thickened, charged with something hotter than tension. Lila’s gaze held a challenge. Nora had once cut into Lila’s palm, peeled back skin to save her fingers from a drunk’s knife. Lila had woken up with stitches and a debt she couldn’t repay.

Graves held up the scalpel. "This weapon was recovered from Dr. Voss’s office. You know her?"

Lila didn’t blink. "I know of her. I’ve heard the name."

"Have you ever been treated by her?"

Lila’s lips curled. "I’ve been treated. By surgeons. By butchers. By men who didn’t wash their hands."

Graves stepped closer. "Did you see her on Mercy Row Alley the night Rizzo died?"

Lila’s fingers tightened around the cigarette holder. "I saw her at the Sunset Grill."

Graves’s jaw worked. The scalpel gleamed in his grip. "You’re certain?"

Lila took a last drag, crushed the butt beneath her heel. "I’m certain."

Nora watched the exchange like a chess match. Lila was playing her own game—one Nora hadn’t scripted. The debt wasn’t repaid. It had changed hands.

Graves pocketed the scalpel with a sharp click. "Your testimony will be invaluable."

Lila adjusted the strap of her trumpet case. "I don’t give testimony."

Graves’s smile was thin. "Professional courtesy, Miss Chase. The District Attorney’s office offers protection to witnesses who cooperate."

Lila laughed. It was a sharp, bright sound. "Protection from what? From men who burn evidence in front of reporters?" She gestured to the ballroom. "From men who lecture about civic trust while holding murder weapons?"

Graves’s face darkened. "You’ll change your mind."

Lila met his gaze. "Or I’ll remember what Nora Voss did for me when no one else would."

The silence that followed was a blade between ribs. Nora felt it slice through her own silence. The debt wasn’t repaid. It had mutated. Now it was leverage.

Graves exhaled through his nose. "You’ll regret this."

Lila adjusted her case on her shoulder. "I already do."

She turned and walked toward the exit, heels clicking like a metronome counting down to war. The brass doors swung shut behind her.

Graves stood motionless, then turned back to the room. His voice was ice. "Continue."

Nora watched him for another heartbeat. Then she slipped out the side door, her portfolio tucked under her arm like a shield. The hallway stretched before her, lit by flickering gas lamps. She passed a water closet, its door slightly ajar. Inside, a nurse with red-rimmed eyes scrubbed her hands raw.

Nora paused at the corner. The ballroom’s murmurs faded behind her. She flexed her fingers, feeling the ghost of a scalpel’s weight.

The nurse behind her coughed, a wet, rattling sound.

Nora didn’t turn. She walked on, toward the stairwell, toward the dark. The only sound was the whisper of silk and leather, and the distant echo of a trumpet case tapping against a wall.

Chapter 6

The Sunset Grill’s back stairs smelled of wet wool and cigarette ash. Nora climbed two at a time, her gloved hands brushing the wall for balance. The brass rail was cold through the leather. She didn’t knock.

Lila’s door stood slightly ajar, a sliver of lamplight cutting the gloom. Nora nudged it wider with her shoulder. Inside, a single gas jet hissed over a glass bowl of water, the flame reflecting in the brass mouth of Lila’s trumpet case.

Lila sat on the edge of her cot, one sock pulled halfway on, a ledger spread open across her knees. She didn’t look up.

“You’re late,” Lila said. “Or early. Depends on the clock.”

Nora set the envelope on the dresser. Two twenties, crisp and straight. “Rent’s paid.”

Lila finally glanced up. Dark smudges under her eyes like inkblots. “Who from?”

“Anonymous.”

“You.”

Nora adjusted the collar of her coat. “I don’t need the thanks.”

Lila tossed the sock onto the cot. “I wasn’t going to thank you.”

A silence settled, thick as cigarette fog. Nora took the envelope back, tore it in half, and dropped the pieces into the bowl of water. They curled like dying leaves.

“Now you’re welcome,” she said.

Lila exhaled through her nose, a sound that might’ve been laughter if it didn’t sound like a warning. “You’re not here to patch me up again. I can still play.”

“Not asking for repayment.”

“Then what?”

Nora withdrew a slip of paper from her sleeve, unfolded it. Three words, written in her precise script: *You owe me nothing.*

Lila’s fingers twitched toward the trumpet case, then stilled. “You could’ve left it under the door like a tramp.”

“I could’ve.”

Lila picked up the slip, held it to the lamplight as if checking for watermarks. “You need something.”

Nora didn’t deny it. “A solo. For the right audience.”

“Blackstone ballroom?” Lila’s voice stayed flat. “Where Graves gets his medal?”

“Graves gets his promotion,” Nora corrected. “I get my license back.”

Lila folded the slip into her palm. “You’re asking me to play at his party.”

“Not a party. A ceremony. Forty-eight hours from now.”

Lila stood, the ledger sliding to the floor. “And if I refuse?”

Nora reached into her coat, pulled out a second envelope—thinner, the edges worn. She set it on the dresser beside the shredding money. “This is a list of every patient you’ve ever treated at the Grill. Names, dates, procedures. All cross-referenced with payments to Reginald Langley.”

Lila didn’t touch it. “You’re blackmailing me.”

“Creating leverage,” Nora said. “Same thing in a city that runs on favors.”

Lila’s hands clenched. “You stitch up gangsters for cash, same as I play for theirs.”

“You play because the music owns you,” Nora said. “I cut because the debt owns me.”

Lila’s jaw tightened. She picked up the envelope, tore it open. Her breath hitched at the first name. Then another. She didn’t look up until she’d burned through half the list.

“You’ve had this the whole time.”

“Had what?”

“Leverage over me.” Lila crumpled the paper in her fist. “Since the night you saved my fingers.”

Nora didn’t move. “I didn’t save your fingers for leverage.”

“Then why?”

“The same reason you’re still breathing,” Nora said. “Because the debt wasn’t to me. It was to you.”

Lila stared at the crumpled paper, then at Nora. “And now you’re cashing it in.”

“Not cashing,” Nora said. “Redeeming.”

Lila tossed the shreds into the bowl. They floated, ghostly white. “I play at the ballroom. I testify. What happens to the list?”

“It burns,” Nora said. “You walk away clean.”

“Clean.” Lila tasted the word like something bitter. “You think I want clean?”

Nora shifted her weight. The stairwell creaked below. Someone was coming up. Fast footsteps, uneven. Lila heard it too—her fingers tightened around the trumpet case.

The door burst open before either could react.

Reggie Langley stood there, his derby askew, his breath ragged behind a cheroot. He didn’t look at Lila. His gaze locked onto Nora, wild and desperate.

“They’re coming,” he said. “Feds. With a warrant. They know about the ledger.”

Lila didn’t move. “What ledger?”

Reggie ignored her, stepped farther into the room. His coat smelled of ink and fear. “I burned the original pages, but the copies—Nora, they’ve got everything. Payments, names, the whole damn guild. Graves is going to bury us all in debt and scandal by sundown.”

Nora’s mouth went dry. “You altered the books.”

“To hide your income,” Reggie snapped. “To keep the auditors off your back. But Graves already knows. He’s got a federal agent waiting at the office with a subpoena.”

Lila’s knuckles whitened around the case. “You’re telling me you burned evidence to protect her, and now it’s worse?”

Reggie didn’t answer. His eyes flicked to the dresser, then back to Nora. “I got rid of the originals. But there’s one page left. The one with the Capone payments. I hid it—”

A floorboard groaned on the landing outside. Reggie’s head snapped toward the door. “Too late.”

Footsteps paused outside Lila’s door. A man cleared his throat. “Miss Chase? Police business.”

Lila didn’t move. Nora’s pulse hammered in her throat. She smelled lavender and carbolic acid rising from the water bowl where the rent money had drowned.

Reggie’s voice dropped to a whisper. “Nora, what the hell is that scent?”

The door swung open.

No one stood there.

The hallway was empty. The gaslight flickered. Then the door at the bottom of the stairs slammed shut below.

Reggie exhaled sharply. “Damn it, Nora—”

She cut him off with a sharp gesture. Then she saw it.

On Reggie’s desk, half-hidden under the ledger’s torn cover, lay a single bloodstained piano key. The ivory was cracked down the middle. A smear of red still glossed the broken edge.

Reggie picked it up between two fingers. “What is this?”

Nora stepped closer. The scent hit her first—lavender, carbolic acid. Then the sight: the key was from the Sunset Grill’s old player piano. The one she’d played the night Rizzo died.

Reggie held it to the lamplight. “You left this?”

Nora’s throat tightened. “No.”

Lila moved to the desk, her voice low. “Then who did?”

Reggie turned the key over. Scrawled on the underside, in Nora’s own handwriting: *Come find me.*

Reggie dropped the key like it burned him. “You’re framing yourself.”

Nora didn’t answer. She was looking at the water bowl.

The two twenties were gone. The shreds had vanished.

Only the lavender scent remained.

Chapter 7

The Green Canary’s back room smelled of whiskey barrels and stale cigar dust. Graves’ usual table sat empty, the high-backed chair angled toward the bar as if expecting a late patron. Nora slid into the shadows near the coat rack, her coat collar turned up, her gloved fingers brushing the polished wood of a cane someone had propped against the wall. It wasn’t hers, but it would do.

Reggie materialized at her elbow, his derby pulled low, his breath smelling of mints and something sour underneath. “You shouldn’t be here,” he muttered, sliding a glass of bourbon toward her without looking. It left a wet ring on the table.

Nora didn’t touch the glass. “I’m not.”

Reggie exhaled through his nose. “Graves left an hour ago. Federal auditors are crawling all over the precinct like maggots on rot. They’re asking for *you*. Explicitly.”

“They always are.”

Reggie’s fingers drummed the table. “They’ve got the ledger fragments. The ones I thought I’d burned. Some federal bastard named Hargrove is waving them around like they’re Holy Scripture.” He leaned in. “He’s got names. Dates. Your name, Nora. Not just in the margins—right there in black and white.”

“Then I’ll be black and blue by morning.”

Reggie’s jaw twitched. “You’re not listening. They know what you did. The stitches. Rizzo’s ring.” He lowered his voice further. “They know about the piano.”

Nora’s gaze flicked toward the door as the hinges creaked. A Capone enforcer—square jaw, shark’s smile—pushed through the crowd wearing a dark suit that hadn’t seen daylight in weeks. He didn’t scan the room. He zeroed in on the bar, ordered a whiskey neat, then turned just enough to let his overcoat fall open. A nickel-plated revolver rode low on his hip, the handle wrapped in white tape.

He muttered something to the bartender. The man paled and nodded toward the back room’s empty chair.

Reggie’s fingers stilled. “That’s Joey Rizzo’s brother. Sal.”

Nora didn’t react. “I’ve heard.”

Sal Rizzo took his drink to Graves’ table, spun the glass between his palms. He didn’t sit. He just stared at the empty chair as if waiting for someone to fill it. Then he said something low enough that only the bartender heard. The man’s Adam’s apple bobbed before he retreated behind the mahogany.

Reggie’s voice dropped to a rasp. “He’s asking if you’re still out there.”

Nora finally picked up the glass, rolled it between her fingers. The bourbon inside hadn’t been touched. It smelled like regret and bad decisions. “And?”

“And he’s not the only one.” Reggie’s eyes darted to the coat rack, then back to her. “Graves’ table is empty, but his coffee cup sits cold on his desk. The clerks say it was delivered with a dollar bill underneath. Just like you used to leave for him after deliveries.”

Nora set the glass down. “I haven’t left dollar bills in years.”

“No,” Reggie said. “You haven’t.”

A silence settled, thick as the haze over the bar. Then Sal Rizzo turned, his gaze sweeping the room until it locked onto something in the corner. Nora didn’t look. She knew what he saw: a single coffee cup, white porcelain, sitting on the edge of Graves’ desk. A dollar bill folded under the rim.

Sal stood, his chair scraping back. He walked over, picked up the cup, examined the bill. His lips moved as he read something written in ink. Then he turned the cup in his hand, examined it closely.

Nora hadn’t written on the cup. Reggies’ ledger handwriting was precise, but not hers. Someone else had left it there.

Reggie’s voice cracked. “Nora—”

Sal lowered the cup, his smile sharp enough to cut. Then he tucked the dollar bill into his vest pocket.

Nora’s gloved fingers curled into her palm.

Sal Rizzo walked out without looking back.

Reggie’s breath came fast. “You weren’t here. You *can’t* be here.”

Nora stood. “Then I’ll go.”

Reggie grabbed her wrist. “Not like that. Not when he’s holding your damn handwriting.”

Nora pulled free. “It’s not mine.”

“Then whose is it?”

She didn’t answer. She was already moving toward the door, her coat swinging open just enough to show the cane in her grip. The coat rack stood between her and the exit, the cane leaning against it like an afterthought.

Reggie’s voice followed her. “Nora!”

She turned at the threshold, just for a second. Sal Rizzo’s silhouette still stood outside, framed by the neon sign of a nearby jazz club. His hand dipped into his vest pocket, pulled out the dollar bill. He examined it again, then held it up to the light.

Reggie’s voice cracked behind her. “What the hell is written on it?”

Nora didn’t say a word. She walked into the night, her boots clicking against the wet pavement, her gloved fingers brushing the cane’s handle as if it were a scalpel.

The dollar bill was too small for a message. Too flimsy. But as she stepped into the alley’s shadow, she saw him hold it up one last time.

Then he tucked it back into his pocket.

The words weren’t meant for her to see.

They were meant for the one who left it.

The one who thought they could control the storm.

Chapter 8

Reggie Langley stood in the federal auditor’s office, his derby in his hands, his cufflinks loose. The air smelled of wet wool and typewriter oil. Two men in suits leaned against the desk, one flipping through ledgers, the other watching Reggie with the quiet patience of a man who had never learned to blink.

“You understand the severity,” the first man said. He didn’t look up. “Irregular deposits. Missing patient logs. Untraceable cash.”

Reggie cleared his throat. “I run a medical office. We take cash. Always have.”

The auditor set the ledger down. “And the deposits to *your* personal account? Fourteen thousand in six months. No record of services rendered. No invoices. Just cash, Mr. Langley.”

Reggie’s fingers tightened around the brim of his hat. “That’s my business.”

The second man finally blinked. “It’s now *our* business. You’re obstructing an official investigation. That makes it obstruction of justice.”

Reggie’s pulse hammered in his throat. “I don’t know what you want me to say.”

The first auditor closed the ledger with a sharp *thud*. “We want the truth. And if you won’t give it to us, we’ll take your office apart brick by brick until we find it.”

---

The Sunset Grill’s back stairs were slick with rain and old grease. Nora climbed them two at a time, her coat damp at the shoulders, her gloved hand brushing the wall for balance. The door at the top stuck when she pushed, then gave way with a groan. The clinic’s single bulb flickered overhead, casting long shadows across the exam table and the dented instrument tray.

She didn’t turn on the light.

Her fingers found the piano’s fallboard by memory. She lifted it, slid her hand beneath the lowest keys. The ledger was gone. The place where it had rested was smooth, clean, as if nothing had ever been there.

Nora exhaled through her nose.

Then she heard it.

A wet, tearing sound from the corner of the room. She spun, her coat flaring. A knife glinted on the floor, its blade wedged between the baseboard and the wall. The handle was wrapped in black electrical tape. A single string hung from it, swaying slightly, as if something had just been cut free.

She crouched. The string led to a trumpet case, split open along its seam. The instrument inside was ruined—slashes across the bell, the valves bent, the mother-of-pearl inlays cracked. A scrap of sheet music fluttered from the case like a broken wing.

Nora reached for it.

The paper was warm.

She flipped it over. A single line in pencil:

*You shouldn’t have saved her.*

---

The Green Canary’s main floor was a crush of bodies, cigarette smoke curling under the chandelier’s dim glow. Graves stood near the bar, his back to the room, his hat pulled low. A judge in a rumpled suit whispered in his ear, his fingers tapping nervously against a glass of gin.

Nora didn’t approach. She took the stairs to the back room, her cane tapping a steady rhythm against the steps. The door was ajar. Inside, two federal agents rifled through a stack of ledgers on the desk. One held up a page, frowned, then tossed it aside.

Graves’ voice cut through the clutter. “Gentlemen, if you’re here to audit my private life, I suggest you start with the *Green Canary’s* books. I’ve got a receipt for every bottle you’ve drunk tonight.”

The agents didn’t laugh.

Nora stepped into the doorway. The agents turned. One reached for his coat.

Graves didn’t. He just exhaled, slow, like a man who had been holding his breath for years. “Dr. Voss.”

The agents’ hands froze.

Nora smiled. “Deputy Superintendent. I heard you were looking for me.”

Graves plucked a cigar from his vest pocket, rolled it between his fingers. “You’re a hard woman to pin down.”

“And you’re a man who likes his ledgers neat.” She stepped closer. “Though I hear they’re not as neat as they used to be.”

The judge flinched. Graves’ jaw tightened. “Careful.”

Nora tilted her head. “Or what? You’ll summon more federal auditors? More ‘irregularities’? More empty chairs in empty rooms?” She gestured to the ledgers. “You’re drowning in paper, Alden. And paper doesn’t bleed.”

The judge cleared his throat. “I should—”

“Stay,” Graves said without looking at him.

The judge stayed.

Nora’s gaze flicked to the ledgers. “You’re missing one.”

Graves’ cigar stilled. “One what?”

“Page.” She stepped forward, her cane’s tip tapping the desk. “The one that ties you to Judge Whitmore’s little… extracurriculars.” She let the silence stretch. “The one that proves you’re not cleaning up Chicago. You’re just laundering her.”

The judge’s face went the color of spoiled milk.

Graves’ cigar snapped in half. “You don’t know what you’re talking about.”

Nora reached into her coat. The judge flinched. The agents’ hands twitched toward their holsters.

She pulled out a matchbook. Flipped it open. The top page was torn out.

Graves stared at it.

The matchbook read *The Green Canary*.

Nora closed it. “I have a copy. Reggie has a copy. Half the West Side Medical Guild has a copy. You’re the only one who thought burning the original would make it disappear.” She tucked the matchbook away. “Paper doesn’t bleed, Alden. But *people* do.”

The judge stood abruptly. “I need air.”

Graves didn’t stop him.

Nora watched the judge weave through the crowd, disappearing into the smoke. Then she leaned closer to Graves. “Forty-eight hours, Alden. Then your little ceremony at the Blackstone.” She tapped the desk. “I’ll be there. In the front row. With a front-row seat to your downfall.”

Graves’ voice was a blade. “You don’t have forty-eight hours.”

Nora smiled. “We’ll see.”

She turned to leave.

The agents didn’t move to stop her.

The judge was already gone.

Graves’ cigar smoke curled into the air like a question without an answer.

---

Mercy Row Alley smelled of rust and wet newspaper. Nora walked fast, her coat pulled tight, her gloved fingers brushing the brick wall for balance. The snow had stopped, but the pavement glistened, reflecting the flickering neon of the Sunset Grill sign.

At her feet, a single sheet of paper lay half-buried in a puddle. She didn’t see it at first. Her cane’s tip caught it. She stopped.

The paper was damp, the ink bleeding slightly, but the words were legible:

*$5,000 to Whitmore. 3/15/27. Delivery at The Green Canary. 11 PM.*

Nora crouched, peeled it from the pavement. Her fingers trembled just enough to smudge the ink.

*Reginald Langley.*

The signature was Reggie’s.

She flipped it over.

The back was blank.

Then she heard the footsteps.

Light. Quick. Not her own.

Nora stood, tucking the paper into her coat without looking up.

Reggie Langley stepped into the dim light of the alley’s single streetlamp, his derby in his hands, his breath fogging in the cold air.

He didn’t smile.

Neither did she.

“You shouldn’t have come here,” Reggie said.

Nora adjusted her gloves. “You shouldn’t have left this.”

Reggie’s gaze dropped to the paper in her coat. “It’s not what you think.”

“Isn’t it?”

“It’s over,” Reggie said. “I burned the ledger. Burned everything. There’s nothing left to find.”

Nora tilted her head. “Then why are you here?”

Reggie swallowed. “Because I heard the auditors were at the Sunset Grill. And I heard you were too.” He exhaled. “Nora, listen to me. You can’t trust—”

She stepped closer. “I don’t.”

Reggie’s voice dropped. “Sal Rizzo isn’t the only one looking for you.”

Nora didn’t react. “I know.”

Reggie’s fingers tightened around his hat. “Then you know this isn’t over.”

Nora looked past him, down the alley, where the shadows pooled thick and unmoving.

Then she said, quiet:

“I know.”

Reggie followed her gaze. His throat worked.

They both heard it.

A footstep.

Not Reggie’s.

Not Nora’s.

Somewhere in the dark, something moved.

Chapter 9

The Blackstone Hotel’s ballroom shimmered under crystal chandeliers, white linen draped over tables like frozen waterfalls. Nora stepped through the gilded doors, her gloves tight, her cane tapping once against the marble floor. A maître d’ in tails gave her a single assessing glance before nodding toward a private alcove where Alden Graves stood alone, his back to the room, a glass of bourbon untouched in his hand.

“Dr. Voss,” he said without turning. “I was beginning to think you wouldn’t come.”

She stopped just inside the alcove’s edge. “You said it was urgent. And private.”

Graves finally faced her. His suit was charcoal, his tie knotted tight. “I said it was an offer.”

“Offers usually require less secrecy.”

A flicker of irritation crossed his face. He gestured to the seat across the small table. “Sit.”

Nora didn’t. “Tell me what you want first.”

Graves exhaled through his nose. Then he reached into his coat and laid a folded contract on the table. The paper was thick, embossed with an official-looking header. “Your license reinstated. Full immunity. A private practice in the Loop—no more West Side backrooms. All in exchange for names.”

Nora didn’t look at the paper. “Names.”

“Names,” he repeated. “Of others like you. Those who operate outside the law. Those who profit from chaos.”

She tilted her head. “And if I refuse?”

Graves picked up the glass, swirled the bourbon. “Then I’ll have no choice but to conclude that your silence is worth more to you than your practice. That you’d rather see me ruin you than save yourself.”

“And what about Lila Chase?” Nora asked quietly.

Graves stilled. “What about her?”

“She saw the night Rizzo died. She can place me elsewhere.” Nora watched his fingers tighten around the glass. “You’ve spent weeks trying to silence her. Why invite me here now if you already have her?”

Graves set the glass down. “Because you’re the one who matters. Lila’s just an echo. You’re the surgeon. You’re the one who left a man dead on an operating table.” He leaned forward slightly. “But I’m giving you a way out. One that doesn’t involve a witness, a trial, or a noose.”

Nora studied the contract. The ink was fresh, the type sharp. Too neat. Too easy. “And the bloodstained scalpel in my office?”

“A mistake,” Graves said. “One I’m willing to overlook—if you cooperate.”

Nora finally looked at him. “You’re asking me to betray my own.”

“You betrayed yourself the moment you took that oath.” He tapped the contract. “Sign it. Tonight. Then we burn the rest.”

She reached for the contract. Her fingers brushed the paper just as the ballroom doors opened behind her.

A burst of trumpet cut through the murmurs of the crowd. The melody was slow, deliberate—Lila Chase’s solo. Nora didn’t turn, but she felt the air shift. The room silenced around the music, every head turning toward the grand piano where Lila stood, her trumpet gleaming under the lights, her fingers steady on the keys.

Graves noticed Nora’s stillness. “You know her.”

Nora kept her eyes on the contract. “I know of her.”

“Funny,” Graves said, watching Lila’s hands move across the piano keys. “She’s been the one thing in this city that moves when everyone else is standing still.” He leaned back. “Ironic, isn’t it? The woman you saved is the only one who can destroy you.”

Nora didn’t answer. She traced the edge of the contract with her fingertip, feeling the embossing. Then she looked up at Graves. “And if I don’t sign?”

His smile was thin. “Then I’ll have to ask Miss Chase to describe that night in detail. The angle of the light. The scent of antiseptic. The sound of your voice.”

Nora’s pulse didn’t betray her, but her grip on the cane tightened. “She won’t.”

“She already has,” Graves said. “To a federal agent. This morning.”

Lila’s solo swelled, the notes trembling through the ballroom like a held breath. Nora’s gaze flicked to Lila’s hands—moving across the piano keys with eerie precision, fingers stretching, retracting, repeating a pattern Nora recognized instantly.

The same pattern Lila’s hands had made the night Nora saved her fingers in the Sunset Grill clinic.

Graves noticed her focus. “What is it?”

Nora didn’t answer. She watched as Lila’s left hand pressed down on a low B flat, then slid up the keys in a perfect chromatic run—*exactly* how the inspector’s notes had described Lila’s hand movements the night of the shooting.

Graves followed her stare. “You recognize the piece?”

Nora’s throat was dry. “She’s playing my song.”

Graves blinked. “Your song?”

“The one I wrote for her.” Nora’s voice was quiet, almost lost under the music. “The one she never heard.”

Graves frowned. Then comprehension dawned. “You composed it?”

Nora didn’t answer. She was watching Lila’s hands, counting the beats, tracking the sequence. The notes matched the inspector’s report perfectly—*to the millisecond*.

Graves exhaled. “For God’s sake, Voss, what are you—”

Nora jerked the contract off the table, crumpling it in her fist. “I’m not signing anything.”

Graves’ face darkened. “You’re making a mistake.”

“No,” Nora said. “I’m making a choice.”

She turned and walked away, her cane tapping a sharp rhythm against the marble, each step deliberate, unhurried. The ballroom seemed to shift around her, guests parting like water, their whispers trailing in her wake.

Graves didn’t call after her. Instead, he picked up his untouched bourbon and downed it in one swallow, his eyes never leaving Lila Chase at the piano.

Lila’s solo ended. The room erupted in applause. Nora didn’t look back.

She reached the main doors just as a hotel attendant stepped forward to open them. She paused, glancing at the grand piano one last time.

Lila’s eyes met hers across the room.

Time stilled.

Then Lila lifted her trumpet, not in challenge, but in acknowledgment—her fingers curling around the mouthpiece, her breath steadying, the stage set.

Nora held her gaze for a long second.

Then she stepped into the hallway, the doors swinging shut behind her.

The ballroom’s murmur swallowed her as she walked away. The elevator doors opened. She stepped inside. The attendant asked if she needed the ground floor.

She said, “No.”

She pressed the button for the roof.

The doors closed.

Chapter 10

The rooftop of the Green Canary cut a jagged silhouette against the Chicago sky. Wind slashed across the tar-paper expanse, rifling through the sleeves of Nora’s coat. She braced a gloved hand against the parapet, squinting down at Mercy Row Alley, a trench of ice and gaslight three stories below.

Reggie stood in the alley’s mouth, exchanging manila envelopes with a kid in a newsboy cap. The boy vanished, weaving through the shadows toward a mail truck idling at the curb. Reggie lit a cigarette, his breath curling white in the cold.

Nora adjusted the focus on her father’s old brass scope, the one she kept bolted to the fire escape outside her clinic. Through the lens, she watched Reggie’s fingers—steady, precise—as he tucked the last envelope into the boy’s satchel.

“How many?” she asked, not turning.

Reggie exhaled smoke. “Enough. Federal offices, Tribune, the Guild. Even that bastard’s own desk.”

Nora lowered the scope. “And the piano key?”

“Gone.” Reggie flicked ash from his cigarette. “In his in-box with a note—*your ledger, not hers*.”

She nodded once. The coded pages would hit in waves, back-dated to before Rizzo’s death. The piano key—salvaged from the wreck of the Sunset Grill’s grand—bore Graves’ initials etched into the stem. A framed gift, delivered anonymously.

“You’re sure about the timing?”

Reggie crushed the cigarette under his heel. “Federal agent takes delivery at eight-fifteen. Auditor follows at eight-thirty. By nine, the press is at his door.”

Nora watched a patrol car’s headlights sweep the alley. “And Lila?”

Reggie didn’t answer right away. From this distance, Nora couldn’t see his face, but she knew the cadence. He was choosing his words.

“She’s at the Sunset,” he said finally. “Playing scales. Like nothing’s wrong.”

Nora tightened her grip on the scope. “She knows the piece?”

“She wrote it.” Reggie’s voice carried a flicker of something—doubt, maybe. “Her composition. ‘Voss Requiem.’”

Nora exhaled through her nose. Lila had titled it after the surgery. The irony wasn’t lost.

She handed the scope to Reggie. “Watch the alley.”

He took it, adjusted the focus. His breath fogged the lens. “You’re not coming down?”

“Not yet.”

Reggie lowered the scope. “You want me to tell her you’re here?”

Nora didn’t answer. She walked to the edge of the roof, her coat flapping like a crow’s wings. Below, Mercy Row’s gas lamps flickered, their glow pooling in the snow like spilled whiskey. The alley’s far end opened to a courtyard where Lila’s trumpet case sat propped against the brick wall.

A draft rattled the fire escape door. Nora turned.

Lila stood framed in the doorway, her coat damp at the shoulders, her fingers curled around the trumpet’s bell. She didn’t smile. Didn’t speak.

Nora held her gaze. “You’re practicing.”

“Always.” Lila’s voice was rough, unused. “Before the crowd.”

Nora stepped closer. The roof’s gravel crunched under her boots. “Even with everything happening?”

Lila’s knuckles whitened on the trumpet. “Especially then. Music doesn’t care about warrants.”

Nora almost laughed. Instead, she dug into her coat and pulled out a folded sheet of music. “Play this.”

Lila didn’t take it. “What is it?”

“The original score. The one I wrote for you.”

Lila’s throat worked. She set the trumpet down, careful, like it might shatter. “I burned that.”

“Not all of it.” Nora held the sheet closer. “This page survived. One copy.”

Lila didn’t move. From the roof’s edge, Reggie cleared his throat. Nora ignored him.

“You’re going to play Graves’ ceremony,” Nora said. “For me.”

Lila’s laugh was short, sharp. “You mean for the world. The federal boys will be there. The press. Half the city.”

“Exactly.” Nora kept the sheet extended. “Your solo starts at nine-fifteen. That’s when the first envelope hits his desk. When the first headline hits the street.”

Lila looked at the sheet, then at Nora. “What if he calls my bluff? What if he arrests me before I play a note?”

Nora folded the sheet and tucked it into Lila’s breast pocket. Her fingers brushed fabric, then flesh. Lila didn’t pull away.

“He won’t,” Nora said. “Because you’re not his witness. You’re his alibi.”

Lila’s breath hitched. “What?”

Nora stepped back. “The night Rizzo died, you were playing this piece. You told the inspector the tempo was 72 beats per minute. The sheet music confirms it. The metronome in the club’s office still has the needle set to 72. You were there, Lila. Not in Mercy Row.”

Lila’s hand hovered over her pocket, pressing the music against her ribs. “You’re saying Graves already knows?”

“Graves thinks he knows everything.” Nora adjusted her gloves. “He doesn’t.”

Lila’s eyes flicked to the rooftop’s edge where Reggie watched, then back to Nora. “And when he figures it out?”

Nora smiled, just enough. “By then, it won’t matter.”

Lila’s trumpet gleamed in the lamplight. She picked it up, thumbs brushing the valves. “If this goes wrong—”

“It won’t,” Nora said. “But if it does, play the solo louder.”

Lila’s mouth quirked. Almost a smile. She lifted the trumpet to her lips, eyes never leaving Nora’s. Then she played a single, clear note—middle C.

Reggie lowered the scope. “Package just rolled past Mercy Row. Headed west.”

Nora nodded. “Stay on the roof. Watch the alley.”

Reggie didn’t ask why. He knew when to speak and when to wait.

Lila’s scale climbed, rising through the octaves, her fingers stretching, retreating, a rhythm Nora had written into muscle memory years ago. The music carried over the rooftops, threading through the city’s winter hum.

Nora turned away before the last note faded. She walked to the fire escape door and paused, her gloved hand on the rail.

“Nora.”

Lila’s voice stopped her.

She turned.

Lila lowered the trumpet. Her breath fogged in the cold. “The bloodstained scalpel in your office—”

Nora held her gaze.

Lila exhaled. “I didn’t want to ask.”

Nora nodded. She stepped through the door. The metal groaned under her weight.

Lila played one more note—a sustained E flat, long and trembling. The sound followed Nora down the stairs, each step swallowed by the music, the wind, the city’s hungry pulse.

Reggie didn’t look up from the scope as she passed. His cigarette dangled from his lips, forgotten.

Nora reached the third floor landing. She paused, pressing her palm against the wall. The brick was cold through her glove.

The trumpet’s final note hung in the air, a held breath.

Then the rooftop door banged shut behind her.

Reggie didn’t see the federal agent step into the alley below, envelope in hand. Didn’t see the agent pause, stare up at the Green Canary’s roof.

The agent knocked on Alden Graves’ apartment door at eight-twenty.

Three floors below the rooftop, Nora quickened her pace toward the stairs. The building’s heat clogged the air, a sickly sweetness of gas and old wood.

She didn’t look back.

Not even when the first headline splashed across the evening edition: *Federal Package Exposes Corruption in CPD*.

Not even when the trumpet’s last note dissolved into silence above her.

Chapter 11

The Sunset Grill’s backroom smelled of cigarette tar and wet wool. Lila Chase sat on a wooden chair, wrists cuffed to the armrests. The cuffs weren’t loose enough to wiggle free, but her fingers still tapped against the wood—a rhythm, a protest, a habit. The federal agent leaning against the doorframe lit a cigarette, exhaling smoke toward the ceiling.

“You’re making this harder than it needs to be,” he said.

Lila didn’t look up. “I already told you what I saw. The woman wasn’t there.”

The agent flicked ash onto the floor. “But you *heard* her. You *know* she was involved.”

Lila’s thumb stilled against the chair. She kept her voice even. “I heard piano music. That’s all.”

The agent pushed off the doorframe and crouched in front of her, his face level with hers. “You’re lying.”

Lila met his eyes. “Am I?”

He grabbed her chin, forcing her head up. “Where were you the night Rizzo died?”

“Playing.”

“Where.”

“At the Sunset.”

“For how long.”

“All night.”

The agent’s grip tightened. “You expect me to believe you were playing *all* night?”

Lila smiled. “I do.”

He stood, stubbing the cigarette into an overflowing tin can. “You’re obstructing justice. That’s a felony.”

Lila tested the cuffs again. No give. “Then arrest me.”

Outside the backroom, the club’s bass drum thudded through the walls, a slow, heavy pulse. Lila’s trumpet case sat unopened in the corner, untouched since they’d dragged her in. The agent turned on his heel and left, the door clicking shut behind him.

Reggie Langley stood in the hallway, his coat draped over his arm, his fedora in hand. The cuffs on his wrists gleamed under the dim bulb.

“You look like hell,” he said to Lila.

She smirked. “You look like a man who’s about to talk.”

Reggie stepped into the room, shutting the door behind him. “They’re asking me about the ledgers. The ones with the extra zeros.”

Lila’s smirk faded. “Whose extra zeros?”

Reggie didn’t answer. He leaned against the wall, crossing his arms. “Graves’ idea, they’re saying. A way to launder his promotion money through the Guild.”

Lila’s fingers curled into fists. “And you believed him?”

Reggie’s jaw tightened. “I signed the checks.”

The backroom door creaked open. The federal agent stood there, a ledger in his hands. “Mr. Langley. We’d like to ask you a few questions downtown.”

Reggie straightened his tie. “Right now?”

“Right now.”

Reggie looked at Lila, then back at the agent. “Fine. But I want a lawyer.”

The agent stepped aside. “You’ll get one. After.”

Reggie followed him out, the door clicking shut. Lila listened to their footsteps fade down the hall, then the stairwell door slam. The Sunset’s music swallowed everything else.

---

Nora watched from the Green Canary’s third-floor balcony, her elbows braced on the wrought-iron railing. The building’s heat leaked through the floorboards, warming her toes through her boots. Below, Mercy Row’s gas lamps flickered like dying fireflies. A squad car idled at the curb, headlights cutting through the snow.

Reggie emerged from the Sunset’s side door, flanked by two agents. His cuffed hands were tucked behind his back, but he walked like he was going to a dinner party, not a police station. He didn’t look up. Didn’t search the balconies. Didn’t look for her.

Nora’s gloved fingers tightened around the railing. She should’ve known he’d crack. Reggie wasn’t built for pressure. Not real pressure. Not like this.

The squad car’s passenger door opened. Reggie ducked his head and slid in, the door slamming behind him. The car’s engine growled to life, tires crunching over ice as it pulled away.

Nora stayed where she was, watching the taillights disappear into the maze of West Side streets. The Green Canary’s balcony was quiet except for the hum of the radiator and the occasional clink of glass from the bar below. She flexed her fingers, feeling the cold bite through her gloves.

Then she saw the limousine.

It rolled past Mercy Row slow, like it was window-shopping, not moving with purpose. The driver’s window was down, and Nora caught a glimpse of Alden Graves’ profile—sharp, unreadable—as the car passed under a lamppost. The limo didn’t slow for the squad car. Didn’t acknowledge it. Just kept going, tires hissing over wet snow.

Nora’s pulse jumped. Graves wasn’t heading to the station. He was heading *somewhere else*.

She pushed off the railing and strode toward the fire escape, her coat flaring behind her. The metal stairs groaned under her weight, the cold biting through the soles of her boots. She took the steps two at a time, her gloved hands sliding along the railing.

By the time she reached street level, the limo was already a block away. She turned left, walking fast, her breath fogging in front of her. The sidewalks were slick with slush, the air thick with the smell of coal smoke and wet wool. She kept her head down, her hands in her pockets.

The limo stopped at the corner of Harrison and Halsted. Graves’ door opened before the car fully stopped. He stepped out, adjusting his coat, and strode toward the entrance of the Chicago Avenue Police Station like he owned the place.

Nora ducked into the doorway of a shuttered tailor’s shop, her back pressed against the cold brick. She watched through the window as Graves disappeared inside, the station’s double doors swinging shut behind him.

A newsboy darted past, shouting headlines about federal indictments. Nora ignored him. Her eyes stayed on the station doors.

She could go in. Right now. Walk through those doors, ask for Graves, and—what? Demand he explain the ledgers? The piano key? The bloodstained scalpel?

Her fingers flexed in her pockets. No. That wasn’t the play.

She needed leverage. Something Graves couldn’t ignore, couldn’t twist. Something that would make the federal agents *listen*.

Her mind flickered to the Sunset’s backroom. To Lila’s cuffed wrists. To Reggie’s quiet confession in the squad car’s backseat.

She needed something else. Something alive.

The station doors opened again. A group of officers spilled out, laughing, their breath steaming in the cold. Graves wasn’t among them. Neither was Lila. Neither was Reggie.

Nora stepped back into the shadows. The limo’s engine idled, waiting.

She thought of the coded ledger hidden in the Sunset Grill’s piano. Of Lila’s trumpet case in the backroom. Of Reggie’s ledgers burning in some incinerator downtown.

She thought of the bloodstained scalpel with the monogrammed ring.

And then she saw the squad car.

It was parked halfway down the block, its rear passenger window rolled down just enough to let out a ribbon of cigarette smoke. The backseat was empty except for a shape slumped against the door—Reggie, his cuffs still on, his coat pooled around his shoulders.

He wasn’t screaming. Wasn’t pounding on the window. Wasn’t doing anything Nora could see.

Then the squad car’s engine growled to life.

Nora’s breath hitched. She stepped forward, her boots crunching over ice.

The squad car’s brake lights flared red as it pulled away from the curb.

Reggie’s head lolled toward the window. His eyes met hers through the glass.

Nora froze.

Reggie’s lips moved. She couldn’t hear the words, but she read them anyway.

*Thank you.*

The squad car turned the corner. The limo’s engine revved, pulling away from the station.

Nora stood in the middle of the sidewalk, her coat flapping in the wind, her gloves clenched into fists.

The city’s winter hum swallowed everything else.

Chapter 12

The Chicago Avenue Police Station lobby smelled of polished marble and wet wool. Nora adjusted her gloves, fingers brushing the record player tucked under her arm. The brass needle gleamed under the chandelier’s glow. Federal agents milled near the elevators, their badges glinting. Reporters jostled for position near the front desk, microphones primed.

Alden Graves stood at the center of it all, his back to the marble wall, a half-smile playing at his mouth. His cufflinks caught the light every time he shifted his weight—once, twice—like he was itching to check his watch but didn’t dare.

Nora walked toward him.

The reporters noticed first. One elbowed another. A pen dropped. The hum of conversation stuttered, then died. Graves turned, his smile slipping just enough to let the cold beneath show.

“Dr. Voss,” he said. “I was starting to think you’d fled the city.”

Nora stopped six feet away. “I don’t flee.”

Graves tilted his head. “No. You don’t. You *hide*.” His gaze flicked to the record player. “What’s that?”

“A reminder.”

The federal agents nearest them turned. One reached for his notebook.

Nora set the record player on the floor and knelt. She lifted the lid, the brass hinges creaking. The needle arm was already positioned over the disc. She depressed the start lever.

Static hissed from the built-in speaker. Graves’ smile vanished.

Then Nora’s voice cut through, cool and clear:

*“Scalpel’s too dull. And the angle’s wrong. You’re going to nick his brachial plexus if you don’t lift the wrist first.”*

The lobby went quiet. The reporters leaned in. Graves’ fingers twitched at his side.

Nora’s voice continued:

*“I told him not to bring a corpse into my clinic.”*

A federal agent cleared his throat. Another took a half-step back. Graves didn’t move. His eyes stayed locked on the record player as the music played on.

*“Scalpel’s too dull. And the angle’s—”*

Graves kicked the record player. The needle skittered, digging a scratch across the disc. The lobby erupted—shouts, scrambling agents, a reporter dropping his notebook.

Graves didn’t look at Nora. He looked at the federal agents, his voice tight. “Secure this area. Now.”

Nora picked up the record player before the agents could react. The disc wobbled in its sleeve, cracked at the center. She cradled it like something fragile.

Graves finally looked at her. “You think this changes anything?”

Nora tucked the record player under her arm. “It changes what they hear.”

Graves stepped closer. His breath smelled of peppermint and something metallic. “You were never there. That recording won’t—”

Nora cut him off. “It’s not where I was.” She tapped the disc. “It’s when he died.”

Graves’ jaw clenched. “You’re digging your grave deeper.”

Nora smiled. It wasn’t warm. “No. I’m making sure it’s not lined with yours.”

A federal agent grabbed her elbow. “Ma’am, you need to come with us.”

Graves held up a hand. The agent hesitated.

Nora didn’t struggle. She let the agent steer her toward the exit, but her eyes stayed on Graves. “Enjoy your promotion speech, Alden. You’ll want to practice your tears.”

The exit door swung shut behind her. The lobby’s voices swelled again, uproarious. Graves stood frozen, one hand pressed to his temple, the other clenched at his side.

Outside, the winter air bit through Nora’s coat. The federal agent hustled her toward a squad car, but she didn’t get in. She turned instead toward the sidewalk, where Lila Chase leaned against a lamppost, her trumpet case at her feet.

Lila’s eyes met Nora’s. No smile. No nod. Just a slow exhale of cigarette smoke.

Nora walked toward her.

Lila flicked the cigarette into the snow. “That’s a hell of a receipt.”

Nora stopped three feet away. “You heard?”

“I heard.” Lila adjusted the strap of her trumpet case. “Sounded like you were giving a masterclass in butchery.”

Nora’s gloved hands tightened around the record player. “I was.”

A federal agent emerged from the station, his coat flapping. He spotted Nora and Lila and headed their way.

Lila didn’t look at him. She looked at Nora. “You’re not out of the woods.”

Nora didn’t blink. “No.”

Lila’s gaze flicked to the station doors, then back to Nora. “Graves isn’t the kind of man who forgets a humiliation.”

The federal agent stopped beside them. “Dr. Voss? We need to process you.”

Nora ignored him. She stared at Lila, unblinking. “I know.”

Lila’s fingers tapped against her trumpet case. Four beats. A rhythm Nora recognized instantly—the intro to “St. Louis Blues.”
