The federal agent cleared his throat. “Now, Dr. Voss.”

Lila’s eyes locked onto Nora’s. “You ever wonder what it’s like to play something beautiful after all this?”

Nora’s pulse jumped. The agent’s grip on her arm tightened.

Lila stepped forward, just enough to block the agent’s view. She lowered her voice. “After the blood. After the lies. After the knives.”

Nora’s throat went dry. “I’ve wondered.”

Lila’s smile was razor-thin. “Good.” She stepped back, reclaiming her space. “Because Graves is going to be very busy tonight. Very *exposed*.”

The federal agent shifted, uncomfortable. “Dr. Voss.”

Nora didn’t move. She kept her eyes on Lila. “And if he isn’t?”

Lila’s smile didn’t waver. She reached into her coat pocket and pulled out a silver flask. Took a swig. Offered it to Nora.

Nora didn’t take it. “I don’t drink on duty.”

Lila shrugged and tucked the flask away. Then she looked past Nora, at the station doors.

Nora turned.

Alden Graves stood in the doorway, his face the color of old parchment. His federal escorts were gone. The reporters were gone. He was alone, his coat flapping in the wind, his hands clenched at his sides.

For the first time, Nora saw the man beneath the badge. Saw the fear.

Then Graves’ eyes met hers.

His gaze dropped to the record player still cradled in her arms. His fingers twitched.

Nora didn’t look away.

Graves’ lips moved. She couldn’t hear the words, but she read them anyway.

*You shouldn’t have done that.*

Nora tilted her head. “I didn’t.”

The federal agent tugged her arm again. “We really need to go.”

Nora let herself be led toward the squad car. Lila fell into step beside her, her trumpet case swinging with every step.

The squad car’s door opened. The backseat smelled of disinfectant and sweat.

Nora got in.

Lila closed the door behind her.

Inside, Nora watched through the window as Graves pressed a hand to his chest, like he’d been gut-shot. The limousine idled at the curb, its passenger door open. Graves didn’t get in. He just stood there, his breath fogging in the cold, his hands trembling.

The squad car’s engine turned over.

As the car pulled away, Nora’s eyes stayed on Graves—on the man who’d thought himself untouchable.

Then the limousine’s headlights flared to life.

They burned brighter than any spotlight.

And in that glare, Nora saw the truth:

Graves wasn’t looking at her anymore.

He was looking at the record player in the squad car’s back window.

He was looking at the disc still spinning inside.

He was looking at the evidence that would unravel everything.

And for the first time, Nora saw the fear turn to something else.

Something desperate.

The squad car turned the corner.

Nora sat back, her gloved hands folded in her lap.

Graves didn’t scream. Didn’t lunge. Didn’t do anything Nora could see.

But she heard the sound anyway.

A single, sharp crack.

Like a bone breaking.

Like a man’s last hope shattering.

Chapter 13

The iron bars of Alden Graves’ holding cell reflected the fluorescent light overhead, sharp and unyielding. Nora adjusted the tilt of her hat, her gloved fingers brushing the coded ledger tucked inside her coat pocket. The station’s holding area smelled of sweat and bleach, the kind of clean that hid rot underneath.

Graves sat on the edge of the cot, his suit jacket draped over the back of the chair. His cufflinks were gone. The sleeves of his shirt were rolled to the elbow, revealing forearms she’d once seen covered in blood from stitching up a broken nose.

He didn’t look up when she entered. “You always did have impeccable timing. Even when it’s terrible.”

Nora stopped three feet from the bars. “I’m not here to chat about punctuality.”

Graves finally lifted his head. The fluorescent light caught the dark circles under his eyes. “Then why are you here? To gloat?”

“To offer you a choice.”

Graves let out a low, humorless laugh. “A choice? After the last two weeks?” He stood, stepping close enough that she could see the faint tremor in his hands. “You’ve already decided my future, Nora. No need to sugarcoat it now.”

Nora didn’t move. “Resign. Tonight. Before the announcement. Take a consulting position in some backwater town. Or stay. Fight the charges. Let the papers dig into every bribe, every ledger alteration, every piece of evidence you planted.” She tilted her head. “I hear Hill County’s jails are lovely this time of year.”

Graves’ jaw tightened. His fingers curled into fists. “You think this is about power?”

“No.” Nora smiled, just enough to show teeth. “This is about justice. The kind you’ve been denying everyone else.”

A federal agent approached from down the hall, his boots scuffing against the linoleum. Graves’ eyes flicked toward him, then back to Nora. “And if I don’t?”

Nora reached into her coat and pulled out the bloodstained scalpel. She held it out, letting the light catch the ring fused into the handle. “Then I show them this. I show them the evidence that proves you murdered a man in cold blood. I show them the ledger Reggie’s been keeping for you. I show them everything.”

Graves didn’t flinch. “You’d burn your own Guild to the ground.”

Nora tucked the scalpel away. “I’m not burning anything. I’m giving you a chance to walk away before I light the match.”

The federal agent stopped beside Nora. “Dr. Voss, you need to step back. He’s still under—”

Nora cut him off with a glance. “I know what he is.”

Graves exhaled through his nose, slow and controlled. “You really think this changes anything?”

“It changes what they hear.” Nora turned toward the exit. “And what they *see*.”

The federal agent stepped forward to escort her out, but Nora held up a hand. She wanted the last thing Graves heard to be his own heartbeat.

The holding area door swung shut behind her. The fluorescent light buzzed overhead. Nora walked down the hall, her heels clicking against the linoleum. The station was quieter now, the reporters and agents gone. Only the hum of the radiator and the distant murmur of voices broke the silence.

She pushed through the station doors into the winter air. The cold bit at her cheeks, sharp and immediate. The sidewalk was empty except for one figure leaning against the lamppost outside the Green Canary, her trumpet case at her feet.

Lila Chase didn’t smoke tonight. Her hands were tucked into the pockets of her coat, her collar turned up against the wind. She looked like she’d been waiting for hours.

Nora stopped beside her. “You didn’t have to stay.”

Lila glanced at the station doors. “Figured you might need a witness.”

Nora didn’t answer. She looked at the Green Canary’s neon sign flickering in the cold—half the letters dead, the other half bleeding red into the dark. The speakeasy’s back door stood slightly ajar, letting out a spill of golden light and the murmur of a piano.

Lila followed her gaze. “You going in?”

Nora shook her head. “Not tonight.”

Lila pushed off the lamppost, her boots crunching in the snow. “Then what now?”

Nora turned toward the street, where a taxi idled at the curb. The driver tipped his hat as she approached. “Now I go home.”

Lila’s fingers brushed the trumpet case’s latch. “Home’s a place with walls, Nora. You don’t do walls.”

Nora opened the taxi door. The interior smelled of leather and old perfume. “I’m rebuilding mine.”

Lila didn’t step back. She leaned against the car door, blocking Nora’s path. “And Graves?”

Nora met her eyes. “He’ll resign by morning.”

Lila’s gaze flicked to the station doors again. Then back to Nora. “That’s it? No trial? No reckoning?”

Nora stepped closer, close enough that she could see the faint scar on Lila’s left wrist—the one Nora’s scalpel had left. “Oh, there’ll be reckoning. Just not the kind he expects.”

Lila’s breath fogged between them. “You’re not afraid of him.”

Nora smiled. It wasn’t warm. “I’m not afraid of the ledger. Or the ring. Or the truth.” She tilted her head. “I’m afraid of what comes after.”

Lila’s fingers tapped against the trumpet case—four beats, slow and deliberate. The rhythm of Nora’s own composition, the one Lila would play in two nights’ time. “And when it does?”

Nora didn’t answer. She got into the taxi. The door shut with a solid *thunk*.

The driver pulled into traffic. Nora watched through the rear window as Lila Chase stood alone on the sidewalk, her coat flapping in the wind, her hands still tapping against the trumpet case.

The taxi turned the corner. Nora’s reflection in the window was pale, her hat casting a shadow over her eyes. She didn’t look away until the Green Canary’s neon sign disappeared from sight.

Then she closed her eyes.

The taxi’s radio hummed static. The driver cleared his throat. “You want some music, lady?”

Nora opened her eyes. “No.”

She reached into her coat and pulled out the bloodstained scalpel again. Rolled the handle between her fingers, watching the fused ring catch the streetlight. The metal was cold. Unyielding.

Just like the ledger in her pocket.

Just like the future she’d carved out of Graves’ mistakes.

The taxi slowed as they approached her boarding house. The street was quiet, the houses dark except for the occasional flicker of a radio behind a drawn curtain. Nora tucked the scalpel back into her coat.

The driver pulled to a stop. “This it?”

Nora nodded. She reached for the door handle.

Then the radio crackled to life.

A voice cut through the static, sharp and official: “All units, suspect vehicle last seen heading west on Roosevelt. 1927 black Cadillac, plates expired. Possible armed and dangerous.”

Nora’s fingers stilled on the door handle.

The driver leaned forward, squinting at the radio. “You know something about this?”

Nora didn’t answer. She rolled down the window, letting the cold air rush in. The voice on the radio continued, listing the vehicle’s description—the same Cadillac Graves had arrived in that first night, the one she’d seen idling outside the Blackstone Hotel.

The driver’s radio crackled again. “Suspect armed with unknown weapon. Approach with extreme caution.”

Nora’s pulse jumped. She looked up and down the street. The boarding house stood dark and silent. No movement. No threat.

Except the Cadillac.

It sat half a block up, its headlights off, engine idling. The streetlamp above it flickered, casting long shadows across the snow.

The driver’s voice was uneasy. “Lady, you sure you want to—”

Nora was already out of the car. Her boots hit the sidewalk with a crunch of snow and ice. The taxi driver shouted something behind her, but she didn’t stop.

The Cadillac’s engine growled low. The headlights flared on.

Nora stood in the middle of the street, her coat flapping around her legs, her gloved hands empty.

The passenger window rolled down.

Alden Graves sat inside, his face pale under the dome light. His tie was loose. His shirt was untucked. His hands gripped the steering wheel so tight his knuckles were white.

Nora didn’t move. “You didn’t resign.”

Graves’ breath came fast, uneven. His eyes were bloodshot. “I gave you a choice.”

“And I gave you one.” Nora’s voice didn’t waver. “This isn’t it.”

The Cadillac’s engine revved. The car lurched forward, tires spitting snow.

Nora didn’t flinch. She stood her ground as the car bore down on her, the headlights blinding.

Then, at the last second, Graves wrenched the wheel hard left.

The Cadillac screeched past her, missing her by inches. It fishtailed down the street, vanishing around the corner in a spray of snow.

The taxi driver stumbled out of the car, his face ashen. “Jesus Christ, lady! What the hell are you—”

Nora didn’t hear him. She didn’t hear the radio crackling behind her. She didn’t hear the driver’s voice, strained and angry.

She only heard the echo of Lila’s trumpet.

The echo of her own composition.

The echo of a choice she’d already made.

Chapter 14

The holding cell smelled of mildew and polished steel. Nora stepped inside without waiting for an invitation. The iron door groaned shut behind her. Alden Graves didn’t look up from the cot where he sat, elbows on knees, hands dangling between them.

He wore no jacket. The cuffs of his shirt were rolled to the wrists, revealing pale forearms marked with old scars. Nora’s fingers brushed the coded ledger in her coat pocket. The fabric whispered against the paper.

“You always did prefer the direct approach,” he said. “Even when it got you into trouble.”

Nora stopped two paces away. The fluorescent light overhead buzzed like a dying insect. “Trouble’s my middle name.”

Graves finally lifted his head. His face was gaunt in the sickly glow, dark crescents beneath his eyes. “You’ve already ruined me in the papers. What more do you want?”

“Your resignation.”

A humorless chuckle escaped him. “You think I haven’t considered that?”

“Not resignation. *Surrender*.” Nora’s gloves were black. She tugged the left one slightly, exposing the wedding band she still wore. “Resign tonight. Take a consulting post in Wichita or Bismarck. Somewhere the federal oversight can’t reach you before the ink dries on your letter.” She tilted her head. “Or stay. Fight the charges. Let them unpick every bribe, every ledger, every planted scalpel. I hear Hill County’s winters are crisp this time of year.”

Graves stood. The cot springs sighed. “You’re offering me a choice?”

“One I never gave the lieutenant who bled out in Mercy Row Alley.”

His eyes flicked to hers, sharp as broken glass. “Careful, Doctor. That sounds like a threat.”

Nora smiled. Just enough to show teeth. “It’s an observation. The same one the jury will make when they see your ledgers, your payments, your *methods*.”

A federal agent appeared at the cell door, his shoes squeaking on the linoleum. “Dr. Voss, you need to step back. He’s still—”

Nora ignored him. “You planted the ring in the scalpel handle. You burned the original ledgers. You left traces in every transaction Reggie handled.” She reached into her coat. The agent’s hand twitched toward his holster. She pulled out a folded document instead. “But you missed this. A copy. In triplicate. Already delivered to three different safe deposit boxes.”

Graves’ jaw worked. “Reggie would never.”

Nora unfolded the paper. Held it up. The agent craned his neck, trying to read the lines. “Reggie Langley’s handwriting? Absolutely. But not his ink. That’s yours. The same pen you used to sign your name on the arrest warrant for me.” She folded it back up. “Funny how memory works when the threat of exposure is breathing down your neck.”

The agent stepped forward. “Dr. Voss—”

“Wait.” Graves’ voice was rough. The agent hesitated. Graves stared at the paper in Nora’s hand. “What do you want?”

“Your resignation. Signed and notarized before midnight. And a public statement retracting all charges against me and the Guild.” Nora tucked the document away. “Or the papers get the full story at dawn. Including how you murdered Sal Rizzo to protect your promotion.”

A muscle jumped in Graves’ temple. His fingers curled into fists at his sides. “You have no proof.”

Nora reached into her coat again. This time she pulled the bloodstained scalpel. Held it up between them, the ring fused into the handle catching the light. “This is proof enough for a hung jury. Add the ledger, the payments, the witness who saw you arrive at the scene—” She let the sentence hang. “Lila Chase plays a mean solo.”

Graves’ breath hitched. Just once. “Lila’s a musician. She doesn’t—”

“She saw you. Heard you order the beating. Watched you plant the scalpel in my office.” Nora twirled the scalpel once, letting the light skate across the metal. “She’s the only reason you’re still breathing, Alden. And she’s the only reason you won’t be breathing much longer if you don’t walk away.”

Graves’ shoulders slumped. For a second, he looked like a tired man, not a predator. Then his expression hardened. “And the Guild? What happens to them when your little secrets come tumbling out?”

Nora slipped the scalpel back into her coat. “They rebuild. With rules written in blood, not ink.” She stepped closer, close enough to see the flecks of gray in his irises. “You wanted legitimacy? I’ll give you chaos. You wanted order? I’ll give you a system where every bribe is a noose. You wanted silence? I’ll give you a choir.”

Graves’ hands were trembling now. Not from fear. From rage. “You’re not a surgeon anymore, Nora. You’re a butcher.”

Nora adjusted her hat. The brim cast a shadow over her eyes. “Funny. That’s exactly what Sal Rizzo said before you put a bullet in his heart.” She turned toward the cell door. The agent stepped aside. “Midnight, Alden. Or I start feeding the press your breakfast.”

The federal agent cleared his throat. “Dr. Voss, I really must insist—”

Nora walked past him without a word. The holding cell door clanged shut behind her. She didn’t look back. Didn’t need to.

The station hallway stretched ahead, fluorescent lights buzzing like angry hornets. Her heels clicked against the linoleum, steady and unhurried. The air smelled of antiseptic and old coffee. A janitor’s mop bucket sat abandoned near the water fountain, the water murky with grime.

She pushed through the station doors into the winter night. The cold hit her like a blade between the ribs. The sidewalk was empty except for a single figure leaning against the lamppost outside the Green Canary. A trumpet case lay at her feet.

Lila Chase didn’t smoke tonight. Her collar was turned up against the wind, her hands tucked into her coat pockets. She looked like she’d been carved from ice.

Nora stopped beside her. “You didn’t have to wait.”

Lila didn’t turn her head. “Figured you might need a witness.”

Nora followed her gaze to the Green Canary’s back door, ajar just enough to let out a spill of gold light and the murmur of a piano. The speakeasy’s neon sign flickered, half the tubes dead, the other half bleeding red into the dark.

Lila finally looked at her. “You going in?”

Nora shook her head. “Not tonight.”

Lila pushed off the lamppost, her boots crunching in the snow. “Then what now?”

Nora didn’t answer right away. She looked down the street, where a taxi idled at the curb, its engine ticking as it cooled. The driver’s hat was visible through the windshield, tipped forward.

Lila’s fingers brushed the trumpet case’s latch. “Nora.”

She turned back. The Green Canary’s music spilled into the night, a trumpet solo weaving through the piano’s chords. It was one of Nora’s compositions. Lila had played it the night Nora stitched up her hand.

Lila’s breath fogged between them. “Graves.”

Nora met her eyes. “He resigns by midnight.”

Lila’s gaze flickered to the station doors. Then back to Nora. “That’s it? No trial? No reckoning?”

Nora stepped closer. Close enough to see the scar on Lila’s wrist—the one Nora’s scalpel had left. The one Lila had turned into music.

“Oh, there’ll be reckoning,” Nora said. “Just not the kind he expects.”

Lila’s fingers tapped against the trumpet case—four beats, slow and deliberate. The rhythm of Nora’s composition. The one Lila would play in two nights’ time. At the Blackstone.

“And when it does?”

Nora didn’t answer. She got into the taxi. The door shut with a solid *thunk*.

The driver pulled into traffic. Nora watched through the rear window as Lila Chase stood alone on the sidewalk, her coat flapping in the wind, her hands still tapping the rhythm against the trumpet case.

The taxi turned the corner. Nora’s reflection in the window was pale, her hat casting a shadow over her eyes. She didn’t look away until the Green Canary’s neon sign disappeared from sight.

Then she closed her eyes.

The taxi’s radio hummed static. The driver cleared his throat. “You want some music, lady?”

Nora opened her eyes. “No.”

She reached into her coat and pulled out the bloodstained scalpel again. Rolled the handle between her fingers, watching the fused ring catch the streetlight. The metal was cold. Unyielding.

Just like the ledger in her pocket.

Just like the future she’d carved out of Graves’ mistakes.

The taxi slowed as they approached her boarding house. The street was quiet, the houses dark except for the occasional flicker of a radio behind a drawn curtain. Nora tucked the scalpel back into her coat.

The driver pulled to a stop. “This it?”

She turned back toward the boarding house.

The front door stood open.

Chapter 15

The Sunset Grill’s stage smelled of cigarette smoke and spilled whiskey. Nora stood at the microphone, her back to the room, the house lights dimmed to a single amber circle. The piano waited behind her, its keys yellowed with age and use.

A hand settled on her shoulder.

She didn’t turn.

“You sure about this?” Reggie’s voice was rough, low. His breath smelled of peppermint and bourbon.

Nora adjusted the brim of her hat. “I’m sure about everything.”

Reggie exhaled through his nose. “Graves won’t show.”

“He’ll show.”

“You want me to watch the exits?”

“Stand beside me.”

Reggie hesitated. Then his fingers brushed the ledger in his inner coat pocket—clean now, rewritten, every bribe and backroom deal scrubbed clean. He nodded once, sharp. “You got it.”

The room quieted. A hush spread like ink on paper.

Lila Chase stepped onto the stage from the wings, her trumpet case tucked under one arm. She wore a black dress cut to show the scar on her wrist, pale against her dark skin. Her fingers tapped the case twice, then stilled.

Nora clasped her hands behind her back. The room held its breath.

Lila reached the microphone. She set the trumpet case at her feet. The clasp snapped open without a sound.

“This one’s called *Quiet Cure*,” Lila said.

The piano seat creaked. A hand—Nora’s—settled onto the keys. The first note hung in the air like a held breath.

Then the room listened.

---

Graves mopped the same station floor where Nora had once stood charged with murder. The linoleum was waxed too often, gleaming under the fluorescent lights. He moved the mop in slow, deliberate strokes, his sleeves rolled to the elbows, his tie loosened.

A officer cleared his throat behind him. “You missing something, Deputy?”

Graves didn’t stop moving. “Just thinking.”

“About the good old days?”

Graves’ grip tightened on the mop handle. “And the bad ones.”

The officer chuckled, walked away.

Graves’ eyes flicked to the station doors. The hinge groaned. A woman in a black coat stepped through, her hat brim casting a shadow over her face.

She paused. Looked at him.

Graves froze. Mop forgotten.

Nora stepped forward. Her gloves were black. The left one rode slightly up, revealing her wedding band.

She didn’t speak.

Graves’ pulse jumped in his throat. He set the mop against the wall. “You look well, Doctor.”

Nora touched the brim of her hat. Just enough to lift it slightly. “I always do.”

A silence stretched between them, thick as varnish.

Graves wiped his hands on his trousers. “You came to gloat.”

Nora tilted her head. “I came to deliver a message.”

“From Lila Chase?”

Nora smiled. Just the corners of her mouth. “From the world.”

Graves’ jaw worked. “The world’s a liar, Nora. Always has been.”

Nora reached into her coat. Graves’ shoulders twitched, but she pulled out a folded newspaper instead of a weapon. She unfolded it, held it up between them.

The front page screamed: *GUILD SURGEON CLEARED OF ALL CHARGES AFTER LANDMARK TRIAL*.

Graves’ breath hitched. “That’s impossible.”

Nora folded the paper. Tucked it back into her coat. “Nothing’s impossible when the evidence is honest.”

“Honest?” Graves’ voice cracked. “You bought judges. Fabricated leads. Threatened witnesses.”

Nora adjusted her hat. “No. I exposed a liar.”

Graves’ hands clenched into fists at his sides. “You’ll regret this.”

Nora stepped closer. Close enough to see the scar beneath his left eyebrow—the one from the night she’d outmaneuvered him in Mercy Row Alley. “I already have. Every time I stitch up a man who trusted me, knowing he’ll go back to the life that broke him.” She tilted her head. “Every time I lie awake, wondering if I could’ve saved him differently.”

Graves’ lips peeled back from his teeth. “You’re a hypocrite.”

Nora didn’t flinch. “No. I’m a survivor.”

A radio crackled on a desk behind them. A voice cut through: “All units, report of an active shooter near the Blackstone Hotel. Possible hostage situation.”

Nora’s shoulders went rigid.

Graves noticed. “What?”

Nora didn’t answer. She walked past him, her heels clicking against the linoleum.

Graves followed. “Nora—”

She stopped at the station’s front door. The night air rushed in, cold and wet. The street was empty except for a lone taxi idling at the curb.

Nora turned. Her eyes met Graves’ for a fraction of a second.

She didn’t look away.

The Sunset Grill’s stage glowed. The trumpet solo rose, mournful and clear, cutting through the room like a blade. The piano answered, the melody weaving through the notes like stitches through flesh.

Lila played with her eyes closed. Her fingers moved with the precision of a surgeon, the precision Nora had once saved.

Reggie stood at the edge of the stage, his arms crossed, his expression unreadable.

Nora watched from the back of the room. The last note hung in the air, trembling, then faded.

Silence.

Lila opened her eyes. She looked at Nora.

Nora didn’t move.

Lila lifted the trumpet to her lips again. This time, the note was different. Sharper. Defiant.

The piano joined. The melody twisted, dark and resolute.

Reggie stepped forward. His hand rested on Nora’s shoulder. “We ready?”

Nora adjusted her gloves. “We’ve been ready.”

The room held its breath.

Lila’s trumpet sang. The piano answered. The two voices wove together, a duet of truth and consequence.

Nora walked toward the stage. The crowd parted. No one spoke. No one breathed.

She stepped onto the stage.

Lila lowered her trumpet. The last note hung between them, vibrating in the air.

Nora removed her gloves. Folded them neatly. Set them on the piano.

Then she reached for the microphone.

The room waited.
